sections.title: The Archaeological Problem
sections.preview: Archaeological investigation of First Contact Era is complicated by institutional forgetting:

sections.title: Category 1: Hybrid Settlement Architecture
sections.preview: **The Sites**: Border regions and neutral zones would have contained mixed human-demon settlements (at least occasionally).

sections.title: Category 2: Tool and Artifact Style Discontinuities
sections.preview: **The Sites**: Border regions and trade posts would have accumulated artifact collections reflecting both populations.

sections.title: Category 3: Settlement Pattern Anomalies
sections.preview: **The Sites**: Border regions would show unusual settlement patterns inconsistent with normal human territorial occupation.

sections.title: Category 4: Dietary Evidence (Remains and Residues)
sections.preview: **The Sites**: Mixed settlements would show dietary evidence of two different populations.

sections.title: Ashford Coexistence Oversight Commission
sections.preview: The Ashford Coexistence Oversight Commission (ACOC) is the first formal cross-kingdom civilian oversight body created specifically to govern human-High Demon coexistence under post-cascade-risk conditions.

sections.title: Purpose
sections.preview: The Commission exists to prevent two failure modes that became visible immediately after disclosure:

sections.title: 1. Oversight of Non-Human Technical Engagement
sections.preview: - Registers and audits all declared High Demon participation in public-risk interventions.

sections.title: 2. Anti-Persecution Safeguards
sections.preview: - Prohibits identity-screening programs based on rumored demonic traits.

sections.title: 3. Crisis Governance Coordination
sections.preview: - Maintains liaison channel to the [[Joint Rift Monitoring Cell]] for imminent-breach exceptions.

sections.title: 4. Public Transparency Layer
sections.preview: - Operates public docket with redaction-limited records.

sections.title: Composition
sections.preview: The Commission uses fixed parity plus independent seats:

sections.title: Founding Constraints
sections.preview: The ACOC was negotiated under severe mistrust:

sections.title: Early Outcomes
sections.preview: In its first quarter, the Commission:

sections.title: Structural Risk
sections.preview: The Commission's legitimacy depends on simultaneously satisfying two oppositional publics:

sections.title: Strategic Significance
sections.preview: The ACOC marks the first durable governance shift after revelation: crisis cooperation has moved from improvised heroics into contested but institutionalized oversight.

sections.title: Ashford Courier Intercept Case
sections.preview: The **Ashford Courier Intercept Case** describes a controlled stop-and-hold operation against a licensed cross-border courier team carrying mixed diplomatic and commercial packets.

sections.title: Event Outline
sections.preview: A two-rider courier relay departed [[Ashford]] with sealed registry packets, trade notices, and low-value contract copies bound for border transfer.

sections.preview: Cross-border courier practice depends on predictable review boundaries:

sections.title: Structural Signals
sections.preview: The case exposed three escalation signals now tracked in courier operations:

sections.title: Immediate Effects on Courier Networks
sections.preview: Within ten days, courier operators implemented changes:

sections.title: Institutional Interpretation Gap
sections.preview: Terravelle-side courier guilds characterized the hold as disproportionate and requested corridor assurances through [[Border Coordination Offices]].

sections.title: Role in Escalation Pattern
sections.preview: Operators now classify this case as a transition marker between Stage 3 and Stage 4 in the [[Near-War Incident Ladder]]:

sections.title: Funding
sections.preview: The Compact is funded by berth fees, bonded-storage surcharges, and private protection retainers from shippers using eastern export lanes.

sections.title: Recruitment
sections.preview: Recruitment comes from dock crews, manifest clerks, low-tier preservation specialists, and former harbor guards who know how cargo disputes escalate.

sections.title: Rules
sections.preview: - manifests filed before berth assignment

sections.title: Opposition
sections.preview: The Compact regularly clashes with smuggling intermediaries, especially groups aligned with [[Black Market Grimoire Circulation]] and opportunists trying to bypass [[Grimoire Authentication]] requirements.

sections.title: What It Trades In
sections.preview: It trades in protection, handling speed, and predictability under pressure.

sections.title: Bedspace as a Commodity
sections.preview: A single room may support multiple workers across alternating shifts. Morning departures free cots for night crews; night departures free cots for day laborers. Boarding operators price by block length, storage allowance

sections.title: Pricing and Access
sections.preview: Bedspace rates rise fastest during cargo surges and border-delay periods when transient labor pools expand. Operators often require upfront payment for multi-day blocks, shifting risk to workers with uncertain next-shift

sections.title: Risk Pattern
sections.preview: The main risk is not homelessness in one moment but rolling displacement. Missing one payment block can cascade into lost sleep, missed shift, reduced income, and further housing exclusion.

sections.title: Informal Governance
sections.preview: Local reputation systems partially regulate abuse. Operators known for fraud or unsafe conditions lose labor-house referrals from hiring intermediaries. This creates minimal accountability but does not prevent exploitati

sections.title: Ashford Emergency Ceasefire Framework
sections.preview: The Ashford Emergency Ceasefire Framework is the provisional bilateral operating agreement established after the first joint stabilization operations in the corridor crisis that followed the [[Kellvost Incursion]] and th

sections.title: Core Purpose
sections.preview: The framework exists to solve a narrow but existential problem: both [[Terravelle]] and [[Auralis]] can still trigger a linked rift cascade through routine military movement if high-tier operations resume under pre-crisi

sections.title: 1. Movement Suspension Window
sections.preview: - Immediate suspension of all high-tier military movement through designated red-band corridor sectors.

sections.title: 2. Technical Verification Requirement
sections.preview: - No major movement order in corridor-adjacent sectors may execute without prior dimensional load assessment from the joint monitoring cell.

sections.title: 3. Joint Incident Reporting
sections.preview: - Both kingdoms must submit real-time rift and micro-distortion observations to shared Ashford registry.

sections.title: 4. Command Relay Continuity
sections.preview: - Three independent relay channels maintained continuously: military, civic, and technical.

sections.title: 5. Personnel Immunity (Technical)
sections.preview: - Temporary cross-border immunity for listed stabilization personnel engaged in corridor preservation tasks.

sections.title: Border De-Escalation Protocols
sections.preview: **Border De-Escalation Protocols** are the practical procedures used by customs offices, guild coordinators, and liaison operators to contain incidents before they become strategic confrontations.

sections.title: Design Principle
sections.preview: A working protocol must satisfy three constraints simultaneously:

sections.title: 1. Technical Clarification Window
sections.preview: When a seizure or hold is triggered by documentation mismatch, both sides enter a fixed clarification window (usually 24-48 hours) before public narrative expansion.

sections.title: 2. Scope Narrowing Rule
sections.preview: Enforcement actions should isolate high-risk packets or cargo lines rather than holding full convoy batches.

sections.title: 3. Reciprocal Transparency Bulletin
sections.preview: Customs offices publish synchronized procedural bulletins listing changed inspection criteria in neutral language.

sections.title: 4. Courier Safe-Lane Commitments
sections.preview: Licensed courier classes carrying administrative and legal continuity packets receive bounded hold-duration guarantees except in declared emergency.

sections.title: 5. Joint Incident Ledger
sections.preview: Both sides log a shared minimal record for incidents crossing agreed severity thresholds.

sections.title: Tier 0–Tier 9 — Exponential Scaling
sections.preview: The numeric tier is the spell's **Flux handling requirement**. Each tier is roughly an order-of-magnitude jump in difficulty, capacity, stability, and precision. Every few tiers feels like a categorically different class

sections.title: Tier 0 — Trace
sections.preview: _Barely any Flux. The threshold most people never bother to formally test._

sections.title: Tier 1 — Flicker
sections.preview: _A small, usable trickle. The first tier where deliberate spellwork is practical._

sections.title: Tier 2 — Working
sections.preview: _The world average. The first tier where magic becomes genuinely useful in daily life._

sections.title: Tier 3 — Load
sections.preview: _A heavy draw. Noticeably taxing for most casters. Where casual use ends and deliberate craft begins._

sections.title: Tier 4 — Strain
sections.preview: _[[Flux]] punishes sloppy work here. Every variable in the sigil needs to be right._

sections.title: Tier 5 — Threshold
sections.preview: _Most people need tools, trained anchors, or prepared sigil support to attempt this safely. The floor of [[Flux Users|Channeler]] territory._

sections.title: Tier 6 — Overload
sections.preview: _Layered stability required. A single unstable variable can cascade the entire cast._

sections.title: Tier 7 — Breakpoint
sections.preview: _Needs prepared sites, Flux reservoirs, or anchored support structures. Cannot be safely attempted in the middle of a field._

sections.title: Tier 8 — Rupture
sections.preview: _Stability is the entire fight. The spell does not forgive any lapse in precision._

sections.title: Tier 9 — Singularity
sections.preview: _Apex tier. [[Flux Users|Conduit]] territory. The kingdom classification system does not formally rate this._

sections.title: Population Distribution (Solumora ~250,000,000)
sections.preview: A person's maximum safe Control Tier is effectively **random**. It does not reliably follow bloodlines, wealth, or nationality. Training improves efficiency and stability, but rarely changes a person's ceiling.

sections.title: Article I - Red-Band Corridor Law
sections.preview: - Permanently designates high-risk corridor sectors as treaty-governed zones.

sections.title: Article II - Joint Technical Authority
sections.preview: - Elevates the [[Joint Rift Monitoring Cell]] from emergency body to treaty institution.

sections.title: Article III - Crisis Intervention Protocol
sections.preview: - Establishes lawful pathway for extraordinary technical intervention, including non-human actors.

sections.title: Article IV - Civil Safeguard Guarantees
sections.preview: - Incorporates anti-persecution constraints: no identity-screening ordinances, no rumor-trigger detention, no association-chain detention expansion.

sections.title: Article V - Market and Logistics Continuity
sections.preview: - Protects essential corridor trade lanes under technical clearance windows.

sections.title: The Detection Problem
sections.preview: [[High Demons]] surviving persecution faced fundamental strategic problem: any characteristic that made them identifiable as demons also made many innocent humans identifiable through false positive matching.

sections.title: Complete Social Dormancy
sections.preview: **Strategy**: Demon completely withdrew from human social integration. Lived in isolation, avoided creation of social ties, eliminated behaviors that would create observation period allowing identification.

sections.title: Concealment Through Institutional Authority
sections.preview: **Strategy**: Demon positioned themselves within institutional structure (religious authority, military rank, administrative role) that gave them legitimate institutional significance beyond their normal authority level.

sections.title: Concealment Through Community Membership
sections.preview: **Strategy**: Demon integrated deeply into specific community (religious community, merchant guild, craft community) that provided protective kinship strong enough to resist identification authorities' pressure.

sections.title: Attempted Normal Integration
sections.preview: **Strategy**: Demon attempted to maintain relatively normal human social position (merchant, craftsperson, family member) while managing characteristic anomalies through careful behavior control.

sections.title: Attempted Rapid Relocation
sections.preview: **Strategy**: Demon relocated frequently to avoid observation period becoming long enough to establish identification pattern.

sections.title: The Institutional Vulnerability Window
sections.preview: All survival strategies depended on specific institutional context:

sections.title: What Surviving Demons Would Demand
sections.preview: Analysis of concealment strategies reveals what surviving demons would rationally demand before accepting revelation:

sections.title: Five Survivors Concord
sections.preview: The Five Survivors Concord is the informal compact formed among the last confirmed [[High Demons]] after terminal persecution conditions in the late [[The Ancient Persecution Era|Persecution Era]].

sections.title: Formation Context
sections.preview: By the final persecution decade, demon survival probability in open society had collapsed. Identification systems, property incentives, and religious-political mobilization had combined into a resilient persecution appar

sections.title: Core Principles
sections.preview: Historical reconstruction across demon memoir fragments identifies five principle commitments:

sections.title: 1. Anonymity Before Influence
sections.preview: No survivor would pursue visible political, religious, or military influence that could trigger renewed detection at scale.

sections.title: 2. Compartmented Identity Lifecycles
sections.preview: Each survivor would rotate identities on long intervals, minimizing pattern accumulation while preserving stable social function.

sections.title: 3. Non-Intervention Default
sections.preview: No direct intervention in major human conflict unless extinction-level dimensional risk exceeded concealment risk.

sections.title: 4. Evidence Preservation Without Publication
sections.preview: Persecution documentation would be retained in private archives to preserve truth without provoking immediate recurrence.

sections.title: 5. Mutual Signal Protocol
sections.preview: If one survivor determined non-intervention had become morally or structurally impossible, a signal cascade would notify others to evaluate coordinated action.

sections.preview: The Concord protected surviving demons and reduced immediate persecution triggers, but imposed a severe ethical burden:

sections.title: Temperature Control ([[Coldbox Seal]])
sections.preview: The most common Flux preservation method: inscribing containers or storage spaces with temperature-reduction effects that slow bacterial growth and chemical degradation.

sections.title: Chemical Stabilization
sections.preview: High-tier practitioners can manipulate food chemistry directly to inhibit spoilage mechanisms.

sections.title: Desiccation Enhancement
sections.preview: Flux-assisted drying removes moisture more completely and quickly than traditional air-drying.

sections.title: Spoilage Detection
sections.preview: Flux sensing allows practitioners to identify spoilage before visible signs appear.

sections.title: Traditional Preservation (Non-Flux)
sections.preview: Flux preservation supplements but does not replace traditional methods. Most food preservation across [[Solumora]] still uses generations-old techniques.

sections.title: Salt-Curing
sections.preview: The most widespread preservation method: salt extracts moisture and creates inhospitable environment for bacteria.

sections.title: Smoking
sections.preview: Hot smoke dries food while depositing preservative compounds from wood combustion.

sections.title: Fermentation
sections.preview: Controlled bacterial growth converts food into stable forms while creating distinctive flavors.

sections.title: Root Cellaring
sections.preview: Cool, dark, humid underground storage extends shelf life for root vegetables, apples, and similar foods.

sections.title: Hybrid Systems
sections.preview: Most effective preservation uses combination of traditional and Flux methods.

sections.title: Household Income and Food Budget
sections.preview: The fundamental equation determining food security.

sections.title: Baseline Calculation: Working-Class Household (Family of 4)
sections.preview: **Household income:** Typical working-class household (two adults, two children) earning 8-15 Watts per person daily across adult earners: 16-30 Watts daily, ~450-700 Watts weekly, ~2300-3600 Watts annually.

sections.title: Seasonal Household Income Variation
sections.preview: Food budget adequacy depends on seasonal employment patterns.

sections.title: Household Coping Strategies
sections.preview: **Surplus accumulation (autumn):** During harvest peak (abundance + peak income), households buy additional grain (if income permits) and preserve goods. Goal: build 4-8 week buffer for winter.

sections.title: Food Work and Household Survival
sections.preview: Food-system employment provides multiple roles within household economies.

sections.title: Family Farming (Small Owner/Tenant)
sections.preview: Seasonal agricultural work (planting, harvest) employs family members. Income: 500-800 Watts annually from farming (small holding of 1-2 acres, yield ~200-400 pounds grain, sales at market ~150-250 Watts plus household c

sections.title: Seasonal Agricultural Labor (Farm Worker)
sections.preview: Harvest employment (8-10 weeks, Harvestfirst-Coolward): 15-25 Watts daily = 600-1000 Watts seasonal income. Off-season employment (winter/spring urban labor): 3-8 Watts daily, 200-400 Watts annual off-season. Total: 800-

sections.title: Urban Day Labor
sections.preview: Consistent urban wage (3-15 Watts daily) provides more predictable income than seasonal agricultural work: 1000-1500 Watts annually, more stable quarterly distribution. Better for household budget planning (no seasonal c

sections.title: Food-Specific Employment
sections.preview: Tavern work (kitchen, service): 15-25 Watts weekly provided moderate income, more stable than seasonal agricultural. Food-sector employment (market labor, fishing, preservation) 8-12 Watts daily provides similar stabilit

sections.title: Housing-Food Trade-offs
sections.preview: Housing and food compete for household budget (together ~70-80% of working-class budget).

sections.title: Rent and Housing Costs
sections.preview: Rent for shared housing: 2-3 Watts weekly (working-class standard). Annual housing cost: 100-150 Watts (3-6% of household budget). Not high but consistent.

sections.title: Housing Quality and Food Budget Trade-off
sections.preview: Cheaper housing (single room, poor ventilation, shared facilities): saves 0.5-1.0 Watts weekly. Saved annual ~25-50 Watts available to food budget.

sections.title: Gender, Children, and Food Labor Allocation
sections.preview: Household member allocation of labor determines both total income and food-preparation capacity.

sections.title: Female Labor Allocation
sections.preview: Primary female task: food preparation and household management (2-3 hours daily), plus childcare. In food-dependent households, additional female labor: preservation work (seasonal, 5-10 hours weekly during autumn), wate

sections.title: Child Labor Allocation
sections.preview: Children in working-class households often split between schooling (growing literacy value, long-term benefit) and wage work (immediate income). Strategic allocation:

sections.title: Debt Cycles and Food Insecurity
sections.preview: Food insecurity creates debt, which perpetuates food insecurity.

sections.title: Winter Deficit and Spring Debt
sections.preview: Household entering winter with inadequate stored surplus must purchase supplemental grain on credit (vendor trust, informal loans, moneylender). Winter credit: estimated 100-300 Watts accumulated by end winter (Darkmoon)

sections.title: Intergenerational Debt
sections.preview: Persistent poverty families accumulate multigenerational debt: parent borrows, dies still owing, child inherits obligation. Documented: some families in permanent debt state (always owe money, never achieve surplus accum

sections.title: Shortage Trigger Patterns
sections.preview: Food shortages emerge from distinct causes, each producing different cascade profiles.

sections.title: Supply-Side Disruption
sections.preview: **Harvest failure:** Poor weather, crop disease, labor shortage, or pest outbreak reduces grain yield below expected volume. Documented failure modes:

sections.title: Demand-Side Surge
sections.preview: **Military mobilization:** War conscription creates enclosed population (soldiers in camps, barracks, depots) with massive food demand. Military procurement diverts supply from civilian markets. Example: Harvestfirst 623

sections.title: Institutional Extraction
sections.preview: **Requisition and taxation:** Government authority requisitioning food for military or strategic reasons. [[Terravelle]] increased grain requisition quota 2626-2630 FE (war preparation); farmers required to supply 15-20%

sections.title: Shortage Cascade Mechanics
sections.preview: Shortage does not distribute evenly; it concentrates through market and social mechanisms.

sections.title: Market Response: Price Surge and Hoarding
sections.preview: When supply shortage becomes apparent, prices rise rapidly. In [[Solhaven]] (autumn 619 FE grain shortage), prices rose 20% day-one, 50% by day-three, 120% by day-seven.

sections.title: Production Labor
sections.preview: Food systems depend on agricultural workers, fishers, and herders.

sections.title: Agricultural Workers
sections.preview: The [[Maren Valley]] produces [[Terravelle]]'s primary grain supply through work by 20,000-30,000 seasonal and permanent agricultural workers.

sections.title: Fishing Labor
sections.preview: Coastal fishing employs 1000-2000 active fishers and 2000-3000 processing workers supporting maritime-based food production.

sections.title: Herding and Dairy Labor
sections.preview: Mountain herding (goat, sheep) and lowland dairy operations employ 500-1000 workers producing milk, cheese, and meat.

sections.title: Processing and Preservation Labor
sections.preview: The transformation of raw material (grain to flour to bread; fish to preserved and salted product; vegetables to preserved forms) requires large labor forces.

sections.title: Grain Processing
sections.preview: Milling, baking, and grain handling.

sections.title: Fish and Meat Processing
sections.preview: Salting, smoking, drying for preservation.

sections.title: Produce Preservation
sections.preview: Drying, salting, fermenting vegetables for winter storage.

sections.title: Quality Control and Inspection
sections.preview: Grain inspectors, weighers, food safety assayers.

sections.title: Cultural Attitudes
sections.preview: In [[Terravelle]], grimoires are treated as practical tools — functional, affordable at the Common tier, and widely distributed. High-tier grimoires exist but are held in institutional libraries rather than private hands

sections.title: The Grimoire Economy
sections.preview: Grimoires are the mechanism by which [[Flux]] capability is distributed broadly rather than confined to high-tier practitioners. An entire economy exists around their production, authentication, distribution, and trade.

sections.title: The Shell
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Dominant hand flat, fingers pressed together, thumb tucked tightly against the palm. The hand is held rigid — no bend at any joint. The back of the hand faces outward away from the body.

sections.title: The Step
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Chosen hand open, fingers spread wide apart, palm facing downward toward the ground. The wrist is bent so the fingers point forward rather than down. Held low at roughly hip height.

sections.title: The Numb
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Dominant hand in a loose fist, but with the middle and ring fingers extended straight forward. The two extended fingers are pressed together. The fist faces inward toward the body, knuckles facing out.

sections.title: The Eye
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Dominant hand with index and middle fingers extended and spread in a V. The other fingers are curled into the palm. The V is held up at eye level, tips of the two fingers pointing upward.

sections.title: The Weight
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Dominant hand in a full fist, thumb wrapped over the outside of the fingers rather than tucked inside. The fist is held with the thumb facing upward. Arm is held close to the body, elbow bent.

sections.title: The Mend
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Both hands required. Non-dominant hand open and flat, palm facing up. Dominant hand placed on top, palm facing down, fingers of both hands aligned in the same direction. The hands are held still, stack

sections.title: The Still
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Dominant hand with all five fingers extended and pressed together, thumb included, forming a flat blade shape. The hand is held vertically, fingers pointing straight up, palm facing inward toward the c

sections.title: The Quiet
sections.preview: **Configuration:** Dominant hand with all fingers curled inward at the second knuckle — not a fist, but a half-curl, as though loosely holding something cylindrical. The thumb rests against the side of the index finger r

sections.title: High Demon Planning and Strategic Organization
sections.preview: [[High Demons|High Demons]] operate according to planning horizons that have no human parallel. Even the youngest have existed longer than kingdoms. The oldest have watched the current world take shape across centuries.

sections.title: The Scale Problem
sections.preview: Human strategic planning typically operates at three scales: immediate (days to weeks), medium-term (seasonal or annual), and long-term (5-20 year projections). This is the natural limit of human careers, institution-bui

sections.title: Individual Planning vs. Coordination
sections.preview: High Demons do not coordinate centrally. There is no council, no consensus building, no shared agenda from which individual strategies diverge. Each demon pursues goals that reflect their own centuries of experience in S

sections.title: Planning Timeframes
sections.preview: The oldest High Demons operate using the following timeframe structure:

sections.title: Planning Methods
sections.preview: High Demons rely on three primary planning tools: **Integration**, **Positioning**, and **Knowledge Development**.

sections.title: Integration
sections.preview: Rather than external manipulation, a High Demon plans by becoming embedded within existing structures. They take on an identity — usually forged but sometimes legitimate — and develop actual position within institutions.

sections.title: Positioning
sections.preview: A High Demon maintains multiple locations of residence or operation simultaneously, often separated geographically and functionally. One identity might be embedded in an [[Auralis]] institution. Another might operate as

sections.title: Knowledge Development
sections.preview: A High Demon with centuries available will develop expertise in specific domains that no human institution can match simply through persistence and repetition. An example would be a demon who has spent 300 years focused

sections.title: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
sections.preview: The primary challenge High Demons face in planning is that their goals often span timeframes so long that major environmental factors are unpredictable. What will kingdoms look like in 300 years? Will [[Flux]] user densi

sections.title: Relationship to Institutional Planning
sections.preview: The five High Demons embedded in Solumora do not compete directly with kingdom institutions for long-term influence. Their goals are orthogonal. A kingdom's 100-year strategic plan aims to achieve specific political, mil

sections.title: Planning Failure Modes
sections.preview: High Demon plans fail in two primary ways: **Assumption Invalidation** and **Visibility Threshold**.

sections.title: Intergenerational Coexistence Curriculum
sections.preview: The Intergenerational Coexistence Curriculum (ICC) is the standardized education framework introduced across participating districts one year after the first casualty to prevent memory drift about persecution dynamics, d

sections.title: Why It Was Created
sections.preview: First-year post-crisis surveys showed a widening generational interpretation gap:

sections.title: 1. Historical Mechanism Literacy
sections.preview: Students study how persecution emerges from procedure, not only ideology, using case modules on [[The Lantern Decrees]] and [[Ashford Witness Amnesty Records]].

sections.title: 2. Risk Communication and Verification
sections.preview: Students learn to interpret public risk bulletins, distinguish rumor from evidence, and understand why technical language precision matters.

sections.title: 3. Cross-Tier Coordination Practice
sections.preview: Scenario modules require mixed-role decision-making where logistics, interpretation, and restraint are scored alongside high-output capacity.

sections.title: 4. Governance Under Uncertainty
sections.preview: Learners evaluate tradeoffs between emergency speed and due-process safeguards through simulated tribunal and oversight hearings.

sections.title: 5. Coexistence Ethics
sections.preview: Framework for non-human civic participation: personhood, accountability, intervention limits, and institutional oversight obligations.

pages.title: Household Food Preparation
outlinks: Urban Food Markets and Daily Consumption
Urban Labor Surge and Slack Cycles

sections.title: Household Food Preparation
sections.preview: The majority of food preparation occurs in households (working-class populations 50-60%, middle-class populations 40-50%), not institutions.

sections.title: Working-Class Household Kitchen
sections.preview: Most working-class households rent portions of shared housing (multiple families per building). Kitchen facilities rudimentary: hearth or small cookstove, water access (shared well or public fountain), minimal equipment

sections.title: Middle-Class Household Kitchen
sections.preview: More spacious kitchen (dedicated room or large shared space), better equipment, water access (household well or internal supply for elite households). More cooking fuel budget.

sections.title: Institutional Food Preparation
sections.preview: Taverns, military barracks, guild houses, [[Ascendant Path]] temples, and institutional kitchens serve 15-25% of food consumption (mostly middle-class and above; working-class occasional).

sections.title: Tavern Kitchens
sections.preview: Most common institutional eating space for working-class populations (reliable cheap meals). Tavern kitchen feeds 50-150 daily customers with limited facilities: large cooking pot, hearth, basic equipment.

sections.title: Military Barracks Kitchens
sections.preview: Military camps and barracks require high-volume food service (3000-5000 soldiers per installation). Centralized kitchen feeding entire installation.

sections.title: Guild and Temple Institutional Kitchens
sections.preview: Larger guilds and [[Ascendant Path]] temples maintain kitchens for feast operations, member events, occasional relief meals. Scale smaller than military but operational infrastructure substantial.

sections.title: Fuel Scarcity and Cooking Capacity
sections.preview: Food preparation entirely dependent on cooking fuel availability.

sections.title: Fuel Types and Costs
sections.preview: - **Wood:** Primary fuel (7-10 Watts per cord, burning 5-7 days per household). Sourced from forest operations or merchant wood suppliers. Winter shortage (demand high, supply weather-dependent, prices 50%+ above summer)

sections.title: Scarcity Cascade
sections.preview: Winter fuel shortage creates direct food-preparation capacity reduction. Common example: Colden-Thawmark 618 FE wood shortage (wet fall prevented proper seasoning; supply inadequate for winter demand). Wood prices spike

sections.title: Cooking Equipment and Technology
sections.preview: Availability and quality of cooking implements affects both labor intensity and outcome quality.

sections.title: Basic Equipment (Working-Class Standard)
sections.preview: - Cooking pot (1-2, shared or individually owned): 5-15 Watts purchase cost

sections.title: Middle-Class Equipment (Enhanced)
sections.preview: - Multiple cooking pots (different sizes, better quality)

sections.title: Institutional Equipment
sections.preview: Professional kitchens with specialized equipment: large cauldrons (heating 100+ gallons), mechanical stirrers, griddles, roasting infrastructure. Cost enormous (500-2000+ Watts for full institutional kitchen), maintained

sections.title: Preservation and Storage Labor
sections.preview: Preservation work happens alongside daily food preparation.

sections.title: Autumn Preservation Surge
sections.preview: Fall (Harvestfall-Coolward) vegetable preservation: drying, salting, fermenting occupies 10-20% of household food-labor capacity. Household members (typically women, sometimes entire family) spend 5-10 hours daily on pre

sections.title: Summer Fish Preservation
sections.preview: Coastal and river communities engage fish preservation (salting, smoking) as summer peak-catch processing. Parallel to vegetable preservation: 4-8 weeks of intense preservation labor.

sections.title: Failure Modes: When Cooking Capacity Breaks
sections.preview: Food systems can have adequate supply but face consumption failure if cooking capacity inadequate.

sections.title: Equipment Failure
sections.preview: Household pot cracks or breaks (common): family must immediately replace (financial emergency) or manage without (cold meals, requiring neighbor borrowing). Institutional kitchen equipment failure (large cauldron breaks,

sections.title: Fuel Interruption
sections.preview: Wood supply shortage (weather, transport failure, price spike): household cooking frequency drops. Populations shift to fewer cooked meals (increasing cold preparation, reducing digestible nutrient extraction). Observabl

sections.title: Cook Labor Loss
sections.preview: Household cook illness/injury (temporary): family management capability drops (alternative cook of lesser skill, time-intensive meal preparation). Institutional cook departure (injury, conscription, death): entire facili

sections.title: Fuel/Water Simultaneous Shortage
sections.preview: Winter water shortage (freezing making water collection difficult) combined with fuel shortage (reduced wood, high prices) creates worst scenario: difficult to cook (limited fuel, low heat), difficult to boil water (slow

pages.title: The Scholarly Reconstruction

sections.title: The Scholarly Reconstruction
sections.preview: Approximately 250-300 years after [[The Ancient Persecution Era|Persecution Era]] ended, both [[Auralis]] and [[Terravelle]] underwent formal historical scholarship review:

sections.title: Did Authorities Accept Lessons?
sections.preview: Both kingdoms formally endorsed scholarly conclusions. Institutional documents show explicit acknowledgment of lessons learned.

sections.title: Lesson 1: Eliminate Informant Financial Incentives
sections.preview: **Scholarly Conclusion**: Financial rewards for informant activity are essential driver of persecution. Removing financial incentives reduces willingness to participate in persecution systems.

sections.title: Lesson 2: Protect Religious Veto Authority Independence
sections.preview: **Scholarly Conclusion**: Religious institutions' veto authority became functionally useless because institutions became invested in persecution continuation. Independence from institutional pressure and funding dependen

sections.title: Lesson 3: Prohibit Property Confiscation as Persecution Tool
sections.preview: **Scholarly Conclusion**: Property confiscation from persecution victims serves as funding mechanism for persecution apparatus and creates economic incentive for continuation.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Create Mandatory Due Process Requirements
sections.preview: **Scholarly Conclusion**: Identifying suspected demons without trial/evidence requirement produced catastrophic false-positive rates. Due process protections prevent persecution.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Mandatory Institutional Accountability Mechanisms
sections.preview: **Scholarly Conclusion**: Institutional authorities who orchestrated persecution faced no prosecution. Lack of accountability enabled authorities to avoid reckoning with institutional failure. Mandatory accountability me

sections.title: Lesson 6: Community Autonomy Protections
sections.preview: **Scholarly Conclusion**: Informant systems and identification frameworks destroyed community trust structures. Community autonomy protections (guild independence, religious community independence, neighborhood privacy)

sections.title: The Institutional Pattern
sections.preview: Why are lessons documented but not implemented?

sections.title: Why Security Matters
sections.preview: Most communication in Solumora is not encrypted. A farmer sending a letter to their cousin in another settlement has no reason to secure the message. But institutions, merchants, and individuals involved in politically s

sections.title: Encryption Methods
sections.preview: Solumora has no standardized encryption. Instead, message security relies on ad-hoc methods that vary by user, context, and threat model.

sections.title: Substitution Ciphers
sections.preview: The simplest method: replace letters or words with predetermined alternatives. A message reading "The shipment arrives on the fifth" might be encoded as "The grain reaches at third bell," where "shipment" = "grain," "arr

sections.title: Book Ciphers

sections.title: Null Ciphers
sections.preview: Real message is hidden within innocuous-looking text. A letter about family news might encode instructions in the first letter of every third sentence. Or the real message appears only in words following specific markers

sections.title: Signal Codes
sections.preview: The [[Desert Signal Network]] uses pre-arranged fire patterns to communicate simple messages: "Emergency at station 7," "Rift Incursion detected," "Request medical assistance." These are not encryption — they are agreed-

sections.title: Flux-Based Encoding
sections.preview: High-tier [[Control Tier|T4-T5]] [[Flux Users]] can inscribe messages into materials in ways that are visible only to other Flux practitioners using specific detection methods. A message written in ink visible only under

sections.title: Practical Limitations
sections.preview: Security is not about perfect protection — it is about making interception and decryption more expensive than the information is worth.

sections.title: Detection and Counter-Measures
sections.preview: Both kingdoms monitor communication for encrypted messages, though their approaches differ.

sections.title: Near-War Incident Ladder
sections.preview: The **Near-War Incident Ladder** is the practical escalation pattern used by mid-level operators in [[Auralis]] and [[Terravelle]] to describe how routine border friction becomes strategic crisis.

sections.title: Why Operators Track It
sections.preview: Policy language treats incidents as isolated events. Operators handling shipments, couriers, and inspection reports see connected causality instead:

sections.title: Stage 1 - Procedural Friction
sections.preview: A customs office changes inspection weighting, paperwork format, or review thresholds. Officials describe this as normal compliance adjustment.

sections.title: Stage 2 - Selective Burden
sections.preview: Specific cargo classes or carriers begin receiving repeated enhanced review without explicit policy declaration.

sections.title: Stage 3 - Incident Signaling
sections.preview: A visible event occurs (seizure, courier hold, convoy turn-back, delayed release) and is publicized as evidence of counterpart bad faith.

sections.title: Stage 4 - Reciprocal Hardening
sections.preview: Counterpart authorities respond with symmetrical or slightly higher friction, usually framed as defensive parity.

sections.title: Stage 5 - Information Fracture
sections.preview: Merchants and administrators stop trusting whether delays are procedural or strategic. Intelligence units absorb customs signals into threat models.

sections.title: Stage 6 - Crisis Threshold
sections.preview: A high-visibility trigger appears (detained officials, convoy confrontation, major seizure claim). Political leadership is forced into public position-taking.

sections.title: Core Drivers
sections.preview: The ladder activates when three conditions overlap:

sections.title: Political Dynamics
sections.preview: Political dynamics in Solumora describe the active tensions, alignments, and power shifts operating within and between institutions rather than static organizational charts.

sections.title: Factional Alignments
sections.preview: Political power in both [[Kingdoms]] organizes around factional structures with distinct objectives competing for influence.

sections.title: Resource Allocation Patterns
sections.preview: Political dynamics are visible in how kingdoms allocate limited resources under competing priorities.

sections.title: Information Access Controls
sections.preview: Political influence depends heavily on information access — who knows what, when, and with what ability to act on that knowledge.

sections.title: Voting and Decision Patterns
sections.preview: Political dynamics in Auralis are most visible through Council voting patterns revealing factional coordination and strategic priorities.

sections.title: Alliance and Betrayal Patterns
sections.preview: Political dynamics include recurring patterns of alliance formation and strategic defection.

sections.title: Wartime Acceleration
sections.preview: Contemporary military preparation is accelerating political dynamics while reducing deliberative space for course correction.

sections.title: Political Intelligence
sections.preview: Political intelligence in Solumora refers to systematically gathered information about power relationships, policy intentions, factional alignments, and institutional decision-making processes.

sections.title: Collection Methods
sections.preview: Political intelligence is gathered through multiple overlapping channels producing different information quality and timeliness.

sections.title: Pattern Recognition
sections.preview: Effective political intelligence requires distinguishing meaningful signals from administrative noise through systematic pattern recognition.

sections.title: Constraints on Use
sections.preview: Political intelligence value is limited by what observers can actually do with information they possess.

sections.title: Intelligence Asymmetries
sections.preview: Different institutional positions and kingdom structures create intelligence collection and actionability asymmetries.

sections.title: Wartime Intelligence Escalation
sections.preview: Contemporary military preparation is expanding political intelligence collection in ways that parallel [[The Three Near-Wars|historical near-war patterns]].

sections.preview: [[Preservation Sigils]] are low-to-mid complexity spell patterns used to slow degradation of food, materials, and sensitive equipment.

sections.title: Selective Disclosure
sections.preview: Both kingdoms control what information becomes public knowledge and when disclosure occurs.

sections.title: Framing
sections.preview: The same event can be described in ways that lead to opposite conclusions. Propaganda works by ensuring the preferred framing reaches populations first and most persuasively.

sections.title: Omission
sections.preview: Information that does not fit preferred narratives is not actively suppressed — it is simply not mentioned. Over time, unreported events disappear from public awareness.

sections.title: Amplification
sections.preview: Information that supports institutional interests is deliberately amplified through repetition, official endorsement, and distribution across multiple channels.

sections.title: Target Audiences
sections.preview: Propaganda is not uniform. Different messages are crafted for different populations, based on what those populations already believe and what behavior the kingdom wants to encourage.

sections.preview: **Advantage:** Deep snow supports sledge transport. Logs can be moved on frozen snow/ice surfaces more easily than wheeled transport on mud.

sections.title: Spring to Autumn Felling (Firstgreen through Coolward)
sections.preview: **Advantage:** Weather tolerance. Frozen equipment hazard absent. Worker conditions manageable.

sections.title: Coastal Fishing Seasonal Reality
sections.preview: Fishing operations follow weather and creature migration patterns rather than administrative calendars.

sections.title: Spring Dangerous Season (Thawmark through Plantmoon)
sections.preview: Storm intensity high, winds unpredictable, water temperature lethal (cold-water shock kills swimmers). Fishing fleet activity drops 50% because risk exceeds financial return.

sections.title: Summer Harvest (Highdry through Peaklite)
sections.preview: Optimal fishing season. Weather cooperative, creature populations stable, fish movement predictable. Fishing fleets fully deployed. Catch volumes highest.

sections.title: Autumn Transition (Harvestfall through Coolward)
sections.preview: Autumn storms begin (cyclone season emerging, winds shifting patterns). Fishing activity remains high because summer accumulated catch requires processing/preservation before winter. Fishing crews work despite rising wea

sections.title: Winter Isolation or Selective Activity (Colden through Thawmark)
sections.preview: Northern fishing operations shut down (ice hazard). Southern fishing (like [[Marn Cove]]) continues at reduced capacity — good crews fish winter storms, poor-quality crews sit idle.

sections.preview: Terrain impassable. Most transport ceases. Operations that must move goods (emergency priority, military supplies) use sledge/snow conditions and accept extreme costs.

sections.preview: Extraction work competes with other seasonal labor (harvest, urban rush, maritime work) for workers. Wage pressure increases when multiple industries need workers simultaneously.

Urban Food Markets and Daily Consumption

sections.title: Food Supply Structure
sections.preview: [[Solhaven]] (population ~30,000-40,000) produces zero food internally. All calories derive from three supply chains.

sections.title: Primary Supply: [[Terravelle]] Grain Through [[Ashford]]

sections.preview: [[Solhaven]] harbor-based fishing (approximately 200-300 active fishers) and coastal trade fishing provides 200-400 pounds daily fish (preserved and fresh). Primary protein source for working-class populations.

sections.title: Tertiary Supply: Cross-Border Specialty Trade

sections.title: Shape (Outer Variable #1)
sections.preview: Shape determines the spell's base [[Flux]] cost and constrains how many other variables can be specified. Each outer point of the shape represents one variable slot. [[Discipline]] always occupies one slot, leaving the r

sections.title: Discipline (Outer Variable #2)
sections.preview: Discipline determines what type of [[Flux]] the spell channels and multiplies the Shape base cost. Each discipline represents a different level of discrimination the [[Flux]] system must maintain.

sections.title: [[Pattern]] (Outer Variable #3)
sections.preview: Pattern defines the geometric shape of the spell's effect in physical space.

sections.title: Reach (Outer Variable #4)
sections.preview: Reach determines the maximum distance between caster and effect.

sections.title: Persistence (Outer Variable #5)
sections.preview: Persistence controls how long the spell's effect continues after casting.

sections.title: Target Spec (Outer Variable #6)
sections.preview: Target Spec defines what the spell affects and how it identifies valid targets.

sections.title: Output Mode (Outer Variable #7)
sections.preview: Output Mode determines what type of effect the spell produces, which may differ from its [[Discipline]]. Natural pairings carry no additional cost. Cross-discipline outputs require additional [[Flux]] precision.

sections.title: Center Core Variables
sections.preview: The center of every sigil contains two variables that do not occupy outer slots: [[Hook]] and [[Mode]]. These define the spell's core action — what it fundamentally does rather than how it reaches or affects its target.

sections.title: Variable Interaction
sections.preview: Variables do not operate independently — they interact to determine the spell's final behavior and cost. A Heat discipline with Sphere pattern and Group targeting creates a zone of temperature control that affects multip

sections.title: Succession Safeguards Charter
sections.preview: The Succession Safeguards Charter (SSC) is a cross-institutional continuity framework adopted one year after the first casualty to prevent rollback of post-revelation protections during leadership turnover.

sections.title: Adoption Context
sections.preview: After six months of relative corridor stability, three risk indicators rose simultaneously:

sections.title: Charter Objectives
sections.preview: 1. Preserve hard constraints on persecution-pattern mechanisms across leadership cycles.

sections.title: Article A - Continuity Trigger Protocol
sections.preview: - Mandatory 30-day overlap for outgoing and incoming officials in designated high-risk offices.

sections.title: Article B - Non-Derogation Floor
sections.preview: - New administrations may tighten safeguards but cannot lower baseline protections without supermajority + public hearing cycle.

sections.title: Article C - Technical Independence Guardrails
sections.preview: - Fixed-term protections for key JRMC and APCT technical seats.

sections.title: Article D - Memory Preservation Requirements
sections.preview: - Annual publication of "near-collapse casebook" excerpts from first-year operations.

sections.title: Article E - Public Alert Integrity
sections.preview: - Risk bulletin language standards cannot be altered by political office without technical concurrence.

sections.title: Enforcement Architecture
sections.preview: The Charter is enforced through linked institutions:

sections.title: Strategic Significance
sections.preview: The SSC shifts coexistence governance from crisis charisma to institutional memory discipline. It acknowledges that stability is not self-sustaining and must be actively re-internalized by each leadership generation.

sections.title: Survivor Benefit Contracts
sections.preview: **Survivor Benefit Contracts** are payout agreements that specify what surviving dependents receive when an expedition member dies, disappears beyond recovery window, or is permanently incapacitated.

sections.title: Contract Structure

sections.title: Payout Models

sections.title: Link to Adjudication

sections.title: Failure and Dispute Patterns
sections.preview: Frequent failure points include:

sections.title: Household Impact
sections.preview: Benefit contracts are one of the few mechanisms that can keep surviving households from immediate debt spirals.

sections.title: Why They Matter
sections.preview: Survivor-benefit design is not only private finance detail. It is social stability infrastructure in high-casualty expedition economies.

sections.title: Self
sections.preview: The spell targets the caster directly. Reliable and low-drift since the caster is always the closest known anchor point for [[Flux]].

sections.title: Individual
sections.preview: The spell locks onto a _specific_ person. Requires the caster to have a clear, deliberate focus on that individual at the moment of activation. Vague intent produces vague results or misfire at higher [[Control Tier|Tier

sections.title: Object
sections.preview: The spell acts upon a specific physical object. Common in utility and crafting applications — hardening tools, heating vessels, sealing containers.

sections.title: Surface
sections.preview: The spell spreads across a flat plane. Used for area treatments, floor or wall effects, and environmental prep work. Pairs naturally with Plane in [[Geometry Pattern]].

sections.title: Group
sections.preview: The spell targets a collection of individuals simultaneously. Requires higher [[Control Tier]] to hold cleanly — the more targets, the more Flux demand. Poorly controlled Group targets tend to clip unintended bystanders.

sections.title: Filter
sections.preview: Rather than targeting something specific, the spell searches for targets that meet a defined variable — material type, Flux signature, temperature, intent. Used in detection, sorting, and conditional effect work. Pairs n

sections.title: Marked
sections.preview: The spell seeks out anyone or anything bearing a corresponding sigil mark. The mark acts as a beacon — range and reliability depend on [[Reach]] and the quality of the mark itself. Commonly used for communication spells,

sections.title: Incident Pattern A: Document Lag Seizure
sections.preview: A convoy with valid papers is delayed by registry update lag. At checkpoint, mismatch triggers seizure. Appeals take weeks. Cargo spoils or is repurposed before ruling.

sections.title: Incident Pattern B: Escort Reclassification
sections.preview: Routes classified as "routine" are reclassified to "elevated risk" without public notice. Existing permits become invalid mid-transit. Couriers are detained.

sections.title: Incident Pattern C: Fee Stack Collapse
sections.preview: Checkpoint fees, compliance surcharges, and correction penalties combine into costs exceeding cargo value for low-margin essentials.

sections.title: Incident Pattern D: Kinship Denial
sections.preview: Families attempting reunification are denied movement based on registry discrepancies rather than threat evidence.

sections.title: The Institutional Capabilities Demonstrated in First Contact Era
sections.preview: First Contact Era institutions proved that coexistence frameworks could:

sections.title: 1. Maintain Separate Governance
sections.preview: **First Contact Era Solution**: Both populations maintained independent internal governance. State-level institutions remained separate; only international contact institutions were shared.

sections.title: 2. Create Shared Institutional Bodies for Contact
sections.preview: **First Contact Era Solution**: Contact Commission, Trade Authority, Scholar Exchange Commission, Border Authority all existed with representation from both populations.

sections.title: 3. Establish Economic Incentives for Continued Contact
sections.preview: **First Contact Era Solution**: Trade was mutually beneficial. Both populations had economic incentive to maintain relationship. Merchants on both sides profited from contact.

sections.title: 4. Protect Religious Authority Independence
sections.preview: **First Contact Era Solution**: Religious authorities in both populations negotiated theological accommodation. Religious institutions were not commandeered by enforcement apparatus.

sections.title: 5. Establish Clear Border and Territory System
sections.preview: **First Contact Era Solution**: Geographic borders were established with both populations recognizing territorial legitimacy of each other. Neutral zones were created for interaction.

sections.title: The Prevention Against Persecution Era Pattern
sections.preview: These institutions specifically prevent [[The Ancient Persecution Era|Persecution Era]] mechanisms:

sections.title: Prevention 1: Prevent Identification Frameworks
sections.preview: **Persecution Era Problem**: Identification protocols enabled systematic questioning and categorization of population for persecution.

sections.title: Prevention 2: Prevent Property Confiscation
sections.preview: **Persecution Era Problem**: Property confiscation created financial incentive structure supporting persecution.

sections.title: Prevention 3: Prevent Informant Systems
sections.preview: **Persecution Era Problem**: Financial incentive for informant activity enabled community fragmentation.

sections.title: Prevention 4: Prevent Religious Opposition Weaponization
sections.preview: **Persecution Era Problem**: Religious opposition movements became political tool for mobilizing persecution.

sections.title: Prevention 5: Prevent Military Action
sections.preview: **Persecution Era Problem**: Military authority was deployed for persecution apparatus.

sections.preview: Unlike [[Drendmark Flux Well]] where ruins form visible geometric pattern, Korden Loop presents as scattered foundation fragments across approximately 2 kilometers of desert surface with no obvious organizing principle.

sections.title: Spatial Anomaly Characteristics
sections.preview: The distortion manifests in three observable patterns, all of which violate basic spatial coherence:

sections.title: Boundary Permeability

sections.title: Flux Characteristics

sections.title: Access and Logistics
sections.preview: Korden expeditions route through [[Korvel Deep Station]] (3 days west of site) or direct from [[Vren Waystation]] (13 days). Korvel routing makes more sense for researchers already working on deep interior ruins projects

sections.preview: Minimal active research. [[The Culmination Faction]] considers Korden low priority. Military applications unclear. Translation efforts focused on higher-value sites.

sections.title: The Lantern Decrees
sections.preview: The Lantern Decrees were a sequence of emergency legal orders issued during years 6-11 of [[The Ancient Persecution Era|Persecution Era]] by municipal-civic authorities in both [[Auralis]] and [[Terravelle]].

sections.title: Origin
sections.preview: After initial demon-identification campaigns produced high panic and low institutional confidence, central authorities faced a practical problem: they could not sustain citywide sweeps without paralyzing trade and civic

sections.title: Mechanism of Harm
sections.preview: The Decrees did not require proof standards equivalent to criminal law. They required administrative confidence thresholds defined by local officials and witness reports.

sections.title: The Week
sections.preview: **6-day weeks** — Five months contain 5 weeks, seven months contain 5 weeks (distributed evenly across year).

sections.title: The Day
sections.preview: **24 hours** — Divided into morning (6am-noon), afternoon (noon-6pm), evening (6pm-midnight), night (midnight-6am).

sections.title: Sundials
sections.preview: **Function:** Cast shadow indicates time by sun position.

sections.title: Lunar Phase Tracking
sections.preview: **Function:** 30-day lunar cycle visible across [[Solumora]] (single large moon with reliable period).

sections.title: Star Position Navigation
sections.preview: **Function:** Specific star patterns rise/set at predictable times, enabling time estimation at night.

sections.title: Water Clocks
sections.preview: **Function:** Controlled water drip marks hours through calibrated container filling or emptying.

sections.title: Mechanical Clocks
sections.preview: **Function:** Spring-driven or weight-driven gears move clock hands, marking hours and sometimes minutes.

sections.title: Personal Watches
sections.preview: **Function:** Miniature mechanical timepiece worn on chain or in pocket. Portable precision time reference.

sections.title: Flux-Oscillation Counters
sections.preview: **Function:** [[Control Tier|T3-T4]] practitioner creates stable oscillating flux field, counts oscillations to measure duration with extreme precision.

sections.title: Time-Binding [[Sigils]]
sections.preview: **Function:** Sigil activates after specified duration, triggering alarm, light, or other signal.

sections.title: Flux-Preserved Time Markers
sections.preview: **Function:** Practitioner creates flux field that degrades at known rate. Field collapse indicates time elapsed.

sections.title: Shadow Length Estimation
sections.preview: **Function:** Observe shadow cast by vertical object (pole, building, person). Shadow length estimates time of day.

sections.title: Work Rhythms and Meal Times
sections.preview: **Function:** Regular daily activities structure time implicitly.

sections.title: Natural Indicators
sections.preview: **Function:** Environmental cues indicate approximate time.

sections.title: Market Structure and Types
sections.preview: Urban food markets operate in three overlapping systems.

sections.title: Public Commodity Markets
sections.preview: **Function:** Bulk staple distribution (grain, preserved fish, salt, dried legumes, root vegetables) where individuals and households purchase daily staples.

sections.title: Merchant House and Guild Procurement
sections.preview: **Function:** Intermediate supply for institutions (taverns, barracks, [[Trade Guilds]], [[Ascendant Path]] temples) and middle-class households.

sections.title: Institutional and Flux-Assisted Retail
sections.preview: **Function:** Specialty foods, preserved goods, Flux-enhanced items, and items preserved by [[Flux Food Preservation]] methods.

sections.title: Daily Consumption Patterns by Class
sections.preview: Food consumption varies dramatically by class, showing how markets allocate scarcity.

sections.title: Working-Class Urban Food
sections.preview: The 50-60% of urban populations earning 3-20 Watts daily spend 40-60% of income on food.

sections.title: Middle-Class Urban Food
sections.preview: The 25-35% earning 20-60 Watts weekly spend 25-40% of income on food.

sections.title: Elite Urban Food
sections.preview: The 5-10% earning 100+ Watts weekly spend 10-20% of income on food (absolute spending equals middle-class budget, but represents fraction of total).

sections.preview: Urban labor demand follows agricultural and trade cycles rather than independent urban rhythms.

sections.preview: **Duration:** 4-6 weeks of maximum emergency intensity

sections.title: Winter Austerity (Colden through Thawmark)
sections.preview: **Duration:** 8-12 weeks of extreme unemployment

sections.title: Seasonal Food Pressure Points
sections.preview: [[Valdenmoor]] experiences distinctive seasonal pattern (different from [[Solhaven]]).

sections.title: Harvest Season Peak (Harvestfirst-Coolward, 8-10 weeks)
sections.preview: Summer abundance: grain arriving daily, prices lowest of year, food security maximum. Populations consume well, store reserves. Working-class populations experience genuine abundance (rare state) — wheat bread accessible

sections.title: Winter Supply Decline (Coolward-Colden-Thawmark-Darkmoon, 12-16 weeks)

sections.title: Spring Scarcity (Thawmark-Firstgreen, 5-6 weeks)
sections.preview: Most acute: stored grain depleted, new crop not yet yielding. Prices peak (80-120% above harvest baseline). Institutions implementing relief. Some populations (vulnerable dependents) face food insecurity.

sections.title: Summer Transition (Firstgreen-Brightward, 5-6 weeks)
sections.preview: New crop beginning producing edible yield (late Firstgreen) — provides relief. Transition from stored to fresh grains. Prices declining. Food security returning.

sections.preview: [[Valdenmoor]]'s agricultural proximity creates graduation in food access, different from [[Solhaven]].

sections.title: Working-Class Food Access
sections.preview: Harvest season: abundant, wages peak, food security high. Post-harvest through spring: constrained. 50-60% of working-class household income dedicated to food. Winter supply decline forces reduction in consumption (move

sections.title: Elite Access
sections.preview: Government, guild leadership, prominent merchants: complete insulation. Food supply non-constraint. Requisition authority or institutional access ensures supply. Food security guaranteed.

sections.title: The Temporary Withdrawal

sections.title: The Permanent Transition
sections.preview: What was intended as temporary became permanent:

sections.title: Reason 1: Religious Opposition Never Declined
sections.preview: **The expectation failed**: Religious opposition movements didn't lose power. Instead, they became institutionalized into mainstream theology.

sections.title: Reason 2: Contact Opportunity Windows Closed
sections.preview: **Initial opportunity**: Early in concealment, humans still remembered contact. Scholars still possessed demon knowledge sources. Institutional frameworks for contact still existed (in memory if not in active practice).

sections.title: Reason 3: Strategic Advantages of Permanent Concealment
sections.preview: **Over time, permanent concealment developed strategic advantages**:

sections.title: The Cost-Benefit Calculation of Permanent Concealment

sections.title: Benefits of Permanent Concealment
sections.preview: - **Complete safety from persecution** (humans can't persecute beings they don't know exist)

sections.title: Costs of Permanent Concealment
sections.preview: - **Complete isolation** (no cross-population interaction, knowledge exchange stopped)

sections.title: Route Notice
sections.preview: This pocket text is intended for couriers, escorts, and route mechanics. It contains only fast-deploy pages that can be cast in poor weather and low-rest conditions.

sections.title: Plate 1: [[Uncommon Grimoire#Pressureseal|Pressureseal]]
sections.preview: Use: Stop container leaks long enough to finish route segment.

sections.title: Plate 2: [[Common Grimoire#Tripwire|Tripwire]]
sections.preview: Use: Camp perimeter warning during short rest halts.

sections.title: Plate 3: [[Common Grimoire#Knocksense|Knocksense]]
sections.preview: Use: Detect movement or impact on locked cargo compartments.

sections.title: Plate 4: [[Common Grimoire#Shove|Shove]]
sections.preview: Use: Controlled push to reset stuck wheel hubs and jammed gate bars.

sections.title: Plate 5: [[Uncommon Grimoire#Shieldwall|Shieldwall]]
sections.preview: Use: Brief barrier against thrown debris during route emergencies.

sections.title: Courier Closing
sections.preview: A repaired axle reaches town. A perfect plan that never moves does not. Cast clean, move fast, file your incidents.

sections.title: Primary Window (Brightward through Harvestfirst)
sections.preview: **Duration:** Approximately 12 weeks of relatively consistent low-rift-activity conditions.

sections.title: Secondary Window (Plantmoon)
sections.preview: **Duration:** 4-5 weeks of moderate-risk-activity conditions (transition period between low and high activity).
